blurb: A tough, silvery-leaved garden hybrid with flat-topped canary-gold flower heads that bloom relentlessly from early summer into fall — xeric, pollinator-friendly, deer-resistant, and at home in Front Range sun and alkaline soil.
spread: Slowly spreading clump; divide every 2–3 years to keep vigor
seasons: Silver-grey ferny foliage from spring through fall; flat golden-yellow corymbs Jun–Sep; foliage semi-evergreen in mild Front Range winters; seedheads add minor winter texture
wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — attracts native bees, beneficial wasps, hoverflies, and butterflies; larval host for some moth species
deer: Deer-resistant; strong aromatic foliage deters browsing; also rabbit-resistant
toxic: Mild — aromatic foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals (wear gloves); toxic to cats, dogs, and horses if ingested in quantity
origin: Garden hybrid; parent species (A. clypeolata, A. taygetea) from SE Europe and W Asia
habitat: Rocky, dry slopes and open grassland in the eastern Mediterranean and Aegean

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8; extremely cold-hardy and reliable on the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). The silvery foliage often remains semi-evergreen through mild Boulder winters, greening up reliably in early spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in-ground in spring (April–May) or early fall (September). Well-drained soil is essential — yarrow planted in wet, compacted clay will rot over winter. Amend heavy clay with coarse grit or pea gravel, or site on a raised bed. In containers, use a well-drained mix; containers dry out quickly in Front Range heat and wind, so check moisture every few days in summer. Overwinter containers in a sheltered spot or sink the pot into the ground with a light mulch over the rim — roots in pots are less cold-hardy than in-ground crowns.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is required for compact growth and maximum bloom — 6+ hours of direct sun daily. Partial shade causes stretchy, floppy stems that require staking, and reduces flower production.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to lean, well-drained soil; tolerates clay if drainage is adequate, sandy and gravelly substrates, and the alkaline pH common to Front Range soils (6.0–8.0). Avoid rich, constantly moist, heavily amended soils — they produce lush, floppy, short-lived plants prone to crown rot.[1][2]
care.water: Low — drought-tolerant once established, typically getting by on 12–15 in of annual moisture. Water moderately during the establishment season (first year); in subsequent years, deep watering every 2–3 weeks during summer heat is usually sufficient. Overwatering is the primary cause of failure.[1][2]
care.spacing: 18–24 in apart; plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form patches 18–24 in wide. Divide every 2–3 years to prevent the center from dying out and to maintain vigorous flowering.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest by division in early spring (April–May) or early fall (September): lift the clump, discard the woody center, and replant vigorous outer sections with roots attached — a reliable and fast method. Also propagates from softwood basal cuttings in early summer. Seeds are viable but 'Moonshine' is a hybrid cultivar, so seedlings may not match the parent (off-type flower color or foliage silvering); division is the preferred method to maintain cultivar characteristics.[1][2]
care.bloom: June through September on the Front Range — a long, canary-gold display of flat-topped corymbs (4–5 in across) held above the silvery foliage. Deadheading spent heads promptly encourages rebloom and extends the season; stop deadheading in late August to allow some seedhead development for minor winter interest and bird value.[1][2]
care.feeding: Minimal — one light application of a balanced, slow-release fertilizer in spring is optional if soil is very poor. On typical Front Range soils, no feeding is needed; excess nitrogen produces floppy stems and reduces flowering.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead regularly throughout summer for continuous bloom. Cut the whole plant back by half to two-thirds after the first main flush (late June–July) to promote a second wave of flowering. In fall after frost, cut stems back to a few inches; the silver basal rosette often persists through winter. Divide every 2–3 years in spring when the center begins to thin or die out.[1]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew in humid summers — ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering. Stem rot in wet or poorly drained soil. Aggressive spreaders in rich, moist soil; lean conditions keep spread modest. Aphids occasionally on new growth. Contact dermatitis possible — wear gloves when handling.[1]
care.companions: Russian sage, catmint, salvia, coneflower, ornamental grasses (little bluestem, blue grama), ornamental alliums, penstemon, and any other full-sun xeric or drought-tolerant perennials; the silver foliage is a superb foil for blue and purple flowers.[1]

edible.summary: Yarrow leaves and flowers are a documented bitter culinary/tea herb used in small amounts, but they contain thujone and other compounds — use sparingly, avoid in pregnancy, and keep it away from pets, to whom it is toxic.[1][3]
edible.parts: The leaves and flowers are the edible parts. They are bitter and are used in small quantities — fresh young leaves in salads, and leaves or flowers brewed as a tea or used to flavor liquors and bitters. This is a flavoring/herbal use, not a bulk vegetable; the bitterness limits the amount used.[1]
edible.preparation: Use leaves and flowers in moderation — a few young leaves raw in a salad, or dried leaves/flowers steeped as a tea or infused into bitters and liqueurs. The strong, bitter flavor means a little goes a long way; cooking or steeping mellows it somewhat. Note that 'Moonshine' is a hybrid garden ornamental selected for flower color rather than for culinary use, so treat any tasting cautiously and in small amounts.[1]
edible.caution: Yarrow contains thujone and sesquiterpene lactones; it should be consumed only in small amounts and is best avoided during pregnancy (it has a traditional reputation for affecting the uterus) and by anyone allergic to other Asteraceae (ragweed, chamomile, daisies). Handling the foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive people, so wear gloves. Critically, yarrow is toxic to cats, dogs, and horses — the ASPCA lists it as toxic (principles include achillein, monoterpenes, and the sesquiterpene lactone achillin), with vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, dermatitis, and depression among the signs — so do not let pets graze it. Be sure of your identification: the finely-dissected ferny foliage distinguishes yarrow from poison-hemlock and other look-alikes with similarly lacy leaves.[1][3]
